slides.title: Who Will Control the AI Infrastructure of Higher Education?
slides.subtitle: From useful tools to structural dependence — and how universities can still shape the terms
slides.author: Dr Stuart Grey
slides.badge: Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026

slides.title: Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI

slides.text: The AI argument in higher education is no longer mainly about whether the tools are impressive. It is about who sets the terms of dependence.

slides.title: From tool to substrate
slides.subtitle: How AI moved from optional assistance to background infrastructure

slides.heading: AI is moving from visible tools to invisible systems
slides.body: The important shift is not simply more campus use. It is that AI is being stitched into teaching, assessment, student support, research workflows and institutional decision-making. When that happens, AI stops looking like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.

points.title: Teaching and assessment
points.description: Generative tools now sit inside drafting, feedback, marking support and redesign of assessment.

points.title: Student support and admin
points.description: Copilots, triage systems and analytics are creeping into advising, helpdesks and routine services.

points.title: Research and governance
points.description: Search, synthesis, coding, grant development and management reporting increasingly run through AI-mediated layers.

slides.heading: Student adoption is already mainstream
slides.body: The governance problem is immediate because student practice has already normalised faster than institutional structures have adapted.

stats.label: of UK full-time undergraduates use AI in at least one way
use generative AI to help with assessed work
say assessment has changed significantly because of AI

slides.heading: Governance is lagging behind everyday use
slides.body: Universities now face a familiar problem: mass uptake at the edge, patchy coordination at the centre, and inconsistent messaging in between. That is a recipe for drift.

points.title: Fragmented policy
points.description: Many institutions are still experimenting with guidance rather than operating settled governance.

points.title: Uneven capability
points.description: Staff literacy, procurement confidence and evaluation capacity vary widely across campuses.

points.title: Ambiguous signals
points.description: Students are told AI matters, but often receive little clear support about how, when or why to use it.

slides.heading: Most infrastructure is invisible until it breaks
slides.body: The easiest way to see infrastructure is through failure. Outages, upstream model changes, or jurisdictional pressure make clear just how much teaching and institutional continuity already depend on remote systems.

points.title: Service continuity
points.description: When cloud-linked platforms fail, content, assignments, grades and communication fail with them.

points.title: Upstream dependency
points.description: A change made by a provider can alter outputs, reliability or availability without the university changing anything locally.

points.title: Sovereignty and resilience
points.description: Once core services sit elsewhere, governance is also about jurisdiction, coercion and the ability to keep operating under stress.

slides.text: Once AI becomes part of the substrate, the question shifts from capability to control.

slides.title: The build-out
slides.subtitle: Why the boom matters even if the hype does not last

slides.heading: Speculative capital is building real capacity
slides.body: The current AI boom is not just hype. It is also an enormous build-out of compute, models, software tooling and enterprise integration. Even if many bets fail, the capacity being built will not simply disappear.

points.title: Compute and cloud
points.description: Data centres, GPU fleets and inference infrastructure are expanding at extraordinary speed.

points.title: Tooling and standards
points.description: Evaluation suites, orchestration frameworks, safety tooling and deployment patterns are being hardened quickly.

points.title: Organisational learning
points.description: The frenzy is forcing universities and vendors alike to learn fast, even through messy pilots.

slides.heading: The upstream build-out is vast — and highly concentrated
slides.body: The boom is leaving behind real infrastructure, but that infrastructure is being financed and controlled by a remarkably small set of actors.

stats.label: top three cloud providers' share of the public-cloud market in 2025
Alphabet's signalled 2026 capital expenditure, largely for technical infrastructure
strategic AI partnerships and GPU commitments shaping the frontier stack

slides.heading: The build-out can leave universities with real inheritances
slides.body: If institutions are strategic, the boom years can amount to a temporary subsidy from speculative capital into durable capability that HE later uses more cheaply and more wisely.

points.title: Cheaper access later
points.description: Competition, commoditisation and maturing open models can drive down cost and increase choice.

points.title: Better institutional tooling
points.description: Standards for VLE integration, evaluation, monitoring and retrieval are improving on someone else's budget.

points.title: More credible alternatives
points.description: Mature open-weight models and sector hosting become more plausible once the ecosystem has been pushed forward.

slides.heading: The boom is also accelerating learning inside universities
slides.body: The real gain is not just hardware. It is the organisational knowledge institutions can build while the ecosystem is moving quickly.

points.title: Policy learning
points.description: Assessment guidance, data rules, DPIAs and usage norms are being stress-tested in real time.

points.title: Pedagogic learning
points.description: Universities are discovering which AI uses support learning and which merely simulate support.

points.title: Capability learning
points.description: Staff are developing literacy in prompting, workflow design, evaluation and critical comparison across tools.

slides.heading: But infrastructure built in a frenzy can still produce dependency
slides.body: Build-outs do not guarantee healthy outcomes. The same boom that creates useful capacity can also create habits of lock-in, misallocated effort and narrow models of progress.

points.title: Lock-in during generosity
points.description: Deep discounts and rapid integrations make dependence feel painless while switching costs are low and still rising.

points.title: Misplaced priorities
points.description: Leaders can chase visible partnerships and shiny features while neglecting pedagogy, people and core digital resilience.

points.title: Capture of norms
points.description: The first systems to scale often end up defining what counts as modern, efficient or inevitable.

slides.text: The boom can be a subsidy for universities — if universities do not confuse access with control.

slides.heading: A small number of firms increasingly control the stack
slides.body: AI is not arriving in higher education as a neutral marketplace of interchangeable tools. It is arriving through a concentrated stack of models, cloud, productivity suites, learning platforms and distribution channels.

points.title: Model layer
points.description: A handful of providers dominate frontier model access and shape the pace of release.

points.title: Infrastructure layer
points.description: Cloud concentration gives the same firms leverage over cost, continuity and technical standards.

points.title: Interface layer
points.description: Existing campus software ecosystems become the channel through which AI is normalised.

slides.heading: Technology does not choose its own social purpose
slides.body: New systems do not automatically produce broad progress. The gains depend on who gets to decide what the tools are for, which kinds of work they replace, and who captures the surplus they create.

points.title: Direction matters
points.description: AI can be used to remove drudgery and expand human capability, or to centralise control and reduce labour.

points.title: Bargaining power matters
points.description: Without strong institutions, efficiency narratives can define the agenda before educators and students get a say.

points.title: Narratives matter
points.description: The story that 'AI is inevitable' can make resistance to bad design sound backward rather than responsible.

slides.heading: In universities, the first question is whose goals are being encoded
slides.body: Most institutional AI projects appear to be about productivity, innovation or student experience. But beneath those labels lie different interests that do not always align.

points.title: Management incentives
points.description: Financial pressure makes cost-saving and scalability tempting default objectives.

points.title: Vendor incentives
points.description: The platform wants adoption, telemetry, stickiness and cross-product integration.

points.title: Academic and student interests
points.description: Universities are supposed to privilege learning, judgment, accessibility, care and public value.

slides.heading: The deepest risk is not only lock-in but agenda-setting
slides.body: Once universities depend on upstream systems, external firms gain quiet influence over what counts as good teaching infrastructure, sensible research workflows and acceptable institutional analytics.

points.title: Research agenda-setting
points.description: Universities increasingly build on proprietary models rather than shaping the architecture, objectives or evaluation criteria.

points.title: Pedagogic agenda-setting
points.description: Teaching begins to bend toward what the tool can easily generate, score or measure.

points.title: Ethical narrowing
points.description: Safety and compliance get more attention than labour, justice, pluralism or democratic control.

slides.heading: The frontier is upstream; universities are mostly downstream users
slides.body: The scale of the companies shaping the AI stack helps explain why universities cannot assume their values will be reflected automatically.

stats.label: of notable AI models in 2024 came from industry
notable models in 2024 were produced by US institutions, far ahead of other national systems
Microsoft reported Azure annual revenue above this level in FY25

slides.heading: Mission drift enters institutions through perfectly reasonable procurement
slides.body: Universities rarely decide to abandon their mission. They drift when sensible short-term deals gradually rewrite the system around external priorities.

points.title: Bundled convenience
points.description: AI arrives inside familiar productivity suites and platforms, making deeper dependency feel incremental.

points.title: Default pathways
points.description: Institution-wide licences quietly define which tools staff and students see as normal or approved.

points.title: Contractual gravity
points.description: Once workflows, content and reporting are built into one stack, future decisions become less free than they appear.

slides.text: If universities plug into the stack on vendor terms, the future arrives as infrastructure before it arrives as debate.
slides.highlight: infrastructure before it arrives as debate

slides.heading: The crucial divide is between augmentation and substitution
slides.body: The most important design decision is not whether AI is present. It is whether AI is used to amplify human expertise or to thin it out.

points.title: Augmentation
points.description: AI can remove admin burden, scaffold formative feedback and support accessibility so staff can focus on judgment and contact.

points.title: Substitution
points.description: AI can also be used to replace teaching presence, automate first-pass support and standardise assessment in thinner ways.

points.title: Institutional test
points.description: Every use case should be judged by whether it improves educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely cost.

slides.heading: The wrong uses are easy to scale
slides.body: Universities under pressure are especially vulnerable to AI uses that look efficient because they industrialise surface tasks while hollowing out the human work students actually value.

points.title: Content at scale
points.description: AI-authored slides, AI-voiced lectures and thin content automation can expand provision while shrinking teaching presence.

points.title: Automated feedback
points.description: Fully automated marking and commentary promise speed but can reduce contestability, nuance and trust.

points.title: Surveillance systems
points.description: Student-success dashboards and risk scoring can become monitoring infrastructure rather than support infrastructure.

slides.heading: The better uses are harder — but more valuable
slides.body: The most defensible AI applications are often the ones that complement human work rather than attempt to replace it.

points.title: Admin relief
points.description: Triage, drafting, search and routine coordination can free staff time for high-value conversation.

points.title: Accessibility and inclusion
points.description: Translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support can widen participation when designed well.

points.title: Formative scaffolds
points.description: AI can support low-stakes practice and feedback that academics then interpret, deepen and challenge.

slides.heading: Pedagogy starts to bend around what the platform can measure
slides.body: Once AI tools are tightly integrated, course design can start adapting to the system rather than the other way round. That is how commercial logic becomes pedagogic logic.

points.title: Standardisation pressure
points.description: Assessments and activities may drift toward formats that are easy to auto-score, summarise or monitor.

points.title: Surface metrics
points.description: Completion, engagement and usage are privileged because they are measurable, even when they are weak proxies for learning.

points.title: Partner visibility
points.description: Platforms and publishers can become the default route through which content, prompts and resources are surfaced.

slides.heading: Data becomes a new rent stream
slides.body: Universities sit on rich corpora of writing, interaction and workflow data. Once AI layers are integrated, those traces become commercially valuable far beyond their immediate educational use.

points.title: Behavioural exhaust
points.description: Prompts, drafts, clicks, revisions and case interactions all become telemetry.

points.title: Institutional dependency
points.description: The model that best 'knows' how your university writes and works may be one you do not own.

points.title: Upstream capture
points.description: Value created from public educational activity can be turned into private rent streams.

slides.heading: Capacity gaps are opening inside the sector
slides.body: The danger is not only that everyone gets the same AI. It is that the best human-plus-AI arrangements go to those with the most money, skill and bargaining power.

stats.label: in HEPI 2025, only this share agreed their institution encouraged AI use
reported institutional AI skills training in the same survey
yet by 2026, AI use of some form was already mainstream among full-time undergraduates

slides.heading: Research and knowledge production are affected too
slides.body: Once disciplines depend on proprietary models, higher education risks shifting from designing and interrogating tools to adapting around opaque systems made elsewhere.

points.title: Methodological narrowing
points.description: Research workflows can default to one model's guardrails, training data and blind spots.

points.title: Reduced pluralism
points.description: Open, local or public-interest models struggle for attention when corporate stacks are easiest to deploy.

points.title: Legitimacy transfer
points.description: Universities can become testing grounds and legitimacy providers for tools they did not shape.

slides.text: The issue is not that AI enters teaching and research. The issue is how much educational purpose gets rewritten by commercial defaults.

slides.heading: The first phase of every platform feels unusually generous
slides.body: Early-stage platform ecosystems often look like public goods. Prices are low, credits are plentiful, integrations are rapid and the user experience is genuinely helpful.

points.title: Subsidised access
points.description: Free tiers, education discounts and pilot credits make experimentation feel costless.

points.title: Rapid value
points.description: Drafting, summarising, coding support and search assistance can deliver immediate productivity gains.

points.title: Soft terms
points.description: Data clauses and dependencies feel abstract because the practical benefits are visible now.

slides.heading: Then the real customer starts to shift
slides.body: As the platform matures, optimisation moves away from students and staff as users toward institutions, intermediaries and upstream business strategy.

points.title: Telemetry value
points.description: Student and staff behaviour becomes raw material for model improvement, analytics and product design.

points.title: Buyer logic
points.description: Enterprise customers are offered dashboards, nudges and metrics that serve management priorities first.

points.title: Behaviour shaping
points.description: The platform starts 'twiddling the knobs' to improve monetisation, retention or partner visibility.

slides.heading: Then the institutional squeeze begins
slides.body: Once the university is deeply integrated, the platform can change the terms of value capture without needing to make the system obviously unusable.

points.title: Price ratchets
points.description: Per-seat AI costs and premium tiers rise as migration becomes more disruptive.

points.title: Paywalled capability
points.description: Features that become pedagogically important move behind higher-priced plans.

points.title: Proprietary dependency
points.description: AI-authored content, tuned workflows and analytics histories live inside hard-to-export systems.

slides.heading: Exit becomes expensive because the institution has already adapted
slides.body: The deepest switching cost is not technical. It is social and organisational. By the time the squeeze is felt, the university has already reorganised itself around the platform.

points.title: Workflow lock-in
points.description: Assessment routines, support scripts, integration logic and approval pathways already assume one stack.

points.title: Capability lock-in
points.description: Staff training, user habits and local expertise become specific to one environment.

points.title: Data lock-in
points.description: Prompts, logs, evaluations and historic outputs are difficult to move or reuse elsewhere.

slides.heading: This is path dependence at institutional scale
slides.body: Early choices about tools, contracts and norms do not stay local or temporary. They accumulate and become the pathway through which later decisions are constrained.

points.title: Standards harden
points.description: File formats, APIs, assessment rules and reporting practices settle around early winners.

points.title: Investment compounds
points.description: Training, integrations and custom workflows make the original choice seem ever more rational.

points.title: Alternatives narrow
points.description: Institutions eventually face not a free choice, but a high-cost break from their own accumulated history.

slides.heading: Architecture is governance, whether institutions notice or not
slides.body: Law and policy matter, but so do the defaults built into code, market structure and everyday norms. Procurement is therefore not a back-office activity. It is a governance choice.

points.title: Code
points.description: Systems decide what is easy, visible, logged, exportable, contestable or impossible.

points.title: Market
points.description: Concentration and bundling shape what appears affordable or realistic.

points.title: Norms and law
points.description: Institutional expectations and regulation matter most when they are translated into architecture and contracts.

slides.text: Preserve the right of exit before convenience becomes culture.

slides.title: A different strategy
slides.subtitle: Engaged stewardship instead of passive consumption

slides.heading: The strongest response is neither refusal nor surrender
slides.body: Universities do not need a fantasy of total sovereignty, and they should not accept passive dependence either. The workable position is engaged stewardship.

points.title: Buy some things
points.description: Use commercial infrastructure where it is genuinely useful and where the terms are governable.

points.title: Build or host selectively
points.description: Keep local or sector-controlled options for high-value or high-risk uses where continuity, privacy or contestability matter most.

points.title: Refuse some things
points.description: Reject deployments that undermine educational purpose even if they look efficient on paper.

slides.heading: Start from public-interest goals, not adoption goals
slides.body: An AI strategy should define what the university is trying to protect and improve before it defines what tools it plans to deploy.

points.title: Human contact
points.description: Use AI to create more time for mentoring, feedback, discussion and care.

points.title: Inclusion and accessibility
points.description: Prioritise translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support.

points.title: Mission guardrail
points.description: Explicitly reject headcount reduction or content automation in place of teaching as primary aims.

slides.heading: People adopt AI through motivation, capability and trust
slides.body: Good strategy recognises that use is not governed only by rules. It is governed by why people reach for systems, whether they know how to use them well, and whether the institution gives them reason to trust the arrangements.

points.title: Motivation
points.description: Time pressure, workload, ambiguity and accessibility needs all drive real-world use.

points.title: Capability
points.description: Training, discipline-specific guidance and verification habits determine whether use is shallow or reflective.

points.title: Trust
points.description: Clear permissions, transparent data rules and reliable tools reduce covert, anxious or inconsistent use.

slides.heading: Universities need a viable system for AI governance, not just a policy
slides.body: Many institutions already have local experimentation. What they often lack is coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system.

points.title: Coordinate the edge
points.description: Connect faculties, services and pilots so AI practice does not fragment into isolated local experiments.

points.title: Build assurance
points.description: Procurement, audit, risk review and evaluation need institutional authority, not just enthusiasm.

points.title: Steer by mission
points.description: Senior governance must articulate what kind of university the AI stack is supposed to serve.

slides.heading: Buy infrastructure like it will one day disappoint you
slides.body: Good procurement assumes that every platform will eventually change price, terms, quality or strategic direction. The question is whether the university remains able to respond.

points.title: Portability
points.description: Require usable export paths for content, prompts, logs, fine-tuning data and configurations.

points.title: Open standards
points.description: Avoid proprietary formats that bury core teaching workflows inside one vendor's stack.

points.title: Exit planning
points.description: Build fallback routes, review points and sunset clauses into every important AI contract.

slides.heading: Treat educational data as a governed asset, not free raw material
slides.body: Student work, support data and interaction traces are mission-governed resources. They should not drift into open-ended upstream model improvement by default.

points.title: Purpose limitation
points.description: Separate service delivery from model training and from research uses.

points.title: No dark training
points.description: Ban open-ended commercial model training on student and staff data without explicit sector governance.

points.title: Oversight
points.description: High-impact data uses need scrutiny from legal, ethical, academic and student perspectives.

slides.heading: Make evaluation a core institutional capability
slides.body: Universities should not rely on product demos, generic benchmarks or marketing claims to decide what is acceptable in teaching and governance.

points.title: Task-based testing
points.description: Build benchmark tasks for feedback, retrieval, advising, accessibility and disciplinary use cases.

points.title: Independent scrutiny
points.description: Review hallucination risk, bias, privacy leakage, citation behaviour and fail-safe performance.

points.title: Continuous review
points.description: Re-evaluate when underlying models, terms or integrations change upstream.

slides.heading: Prefer modular architecture and plural ecosystems
slides.body: The goal is not to build everything locally. It is to avoid allowing one stack to become the only imaginable path.

points.title: Modular design
points.description: Use abstraction layers, retrieval components and portable data structures wherever feasible.

points.title: Plural model exposure
points.description: Ensure staff and students can compare multiple systems, including open or locally hosted options.

points.title: Right-sized models
points.description: For many educational tasks, grounded and well-evaluated smaller systems may be better than the biggest generic model.

slides.heading: Stewardship is a compact but demanding agenda
slides.body: Universities do not need one hundred principles. They need a small number of capabilities applied consistently.

stats.label: core capabilities: procurement, data governance, evaluation, resilience and collaboration
default questions: what if prices rise, quality drops, or the provider disappears?
governing standard: does this strengthen educational purpose and public value?

slides.heading: Sector collaboration and public alternatives matter
slides.body: No single university has enough leverage to negotiate every clause, evaluate every model or build every critical service alone. Collaboration is a practical necessity.

points.title: Shared procurement
points.description: Use sector bodies and consortia to negotiate stronger data terms, exit rights and auditability.

points.title: Shared evaluation
points.description: Build common test suites, model cards and red-teaming practices for educational use cases.

points.title: Public-interest infrastructure
points.description: Explore national, regional or mission-based alternatives where the market produces choke points.

slides.text: The alternative to passive dependence is not heroic self-sufficiency. It is practical stewardship.

slides.title: What the next decade looks like
slides.subtitle: Two futures are still possible

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities play this moment well
slides.body: The optimistic future is not one in which universities beat hyperscalers at their own game. It is one in which they use the boom to build durable, plural and publicly oriented capability.

points.title: Cheaper infrastructure, better terms
points.description: Institutions inherit mature tools and compute without surrendering all bargaining power.

points.title: Human-centred pedagogy
points.description: AI is used to deepen feedback, accessibility and high-value contact rather than thin them out.

points.title: Stronger public capacity
points.description: Sector collaboration creates standards, evaluation routines and alternatives that keep the system contestable.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities get captured by the stack
slides.body: The pessimistic future is not spectacular collapse. It is a quieter form of dependency in which institutions keep functioning while their freedom, pluralism and educational quality erode.

points.title: Rent extraction
points.description: AI becomes a permanent premium layer across teaching, admin, assessment and research workflows.

points.title: Thin provision
points.description: Lower-status students and institutions get automated, standardised provision while wealthier parts of the sector preserve human-rich models.

points.title: Opaque governance
points.description: Research, pedagogy and student support are increasingly shaped by systems the university cannot fully audit or challenge.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: The next few years require three decisions from leaders
slides.body: Every institution does not need the same answer, but all of them need to decide what they will refuse to outsource by default.

points.title: What must we retain control over?
points.description: Define the teaching, assessment, data or support functions where exit, auditability and human oversight are non-negotiable.

points.title: What should we build or evaluate together?
points.description: Identify the areas where sector collaboration creates more leverage than acting alone.

points.title: What rights should students have?
points.description: Make explicit the rights to transparency, contestability, accessibility and non-AI pathways in core processes.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: A Monday-morning agenda for institutions
slides.body: The first moves are not glamorous. They are the foundations of a university that remains able to govern its own AI future.

points.title: Map dependence
points.description: Identify where AI is already embedded in teaching, assessment, support, analytics and procurement.

points.title: Tighten the basics
points.description: Create a cross-functional procurement checklist, clear data rules and one evaluation routine for a high-priority use case.

points.title: Act collectively
points.description: Join or build a coalition for shared bargaining, evaluation or open alternatives before the defaults settle.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.text: Universities are not only deciding whether to use AI. They are helping fix the defaults of higher education for years.
Who will ultimately control the AI infrastructure higher education comes to depend on — and what kind of university will that make possible?
